headline: US medical equipment company Stryker says cyberattack disrupted its global networks.
summary: Stryker, a major U.S. medical equipment company, said a cyberattack disrupted its global networks Wednesday.  Stryker's statement said the cyberattack hit its Microsoft programs.  Stryker is based in Portage, Michigan, and makes a variety of medical products, from artificial joints to hospital beds.

headline: U.S. Medical Company Hit With Suspected Iran-Linked Cyberattack
summary: Medical technology company Stryker is experiencing a global outage across its systems, with staff and contractors reporting that the logo of an Iran-linked hacking group has appeared on login pages.  Staff found that remote devices running Microsoft’s Windows operating system—such as cellphones, laptops and others configured to connect to Stryker’s technology systems—had been wiped.  A successful attack on a major U.S. company would be a significant escalation in a cyber conflict that had been expected to follow the U.S. and Israel’s military campaign in Iran, which began on Feb. 28.

raw.headline: U.S. Medical Company Hit With Suspected Iran-Linked Cyberattack
raw.summary: Medical technology company Stryker is experiencing a global outage across its systems, with staff and contractors reporting that the logo of an Iran-linked hacking group has appeared on login pages.  Staff found that remote devices running Microsoft’s Windows operating system—such as cellphones, laptops and others configured to connect to Stryker’s technology systems—had been wiped.  A successful attack on a major U.S. company would be a significant escalation in a cyber conflict that had been expected to follow the U.S. and Israel’s military campaign in Iran, which began on Feb. 28.

headline: Musk unveils joint Tesla-xAI project 'Macrohard', eyes software disruption
summary: Elon Musk on Wednesday unveiled a joint project between Tesla and his artificial intelligence startup xAI, which ‌he called "Macrohard" or "Digital Optimus" and said that it was a ‌system capable of emulating the functions of software companies.  In a post on his social ​media platform X, Musk said the project pairs xAI's Grok large language model, acting as a high-level "navigator," with a Tesla-developed AI agent that processes real-time computer screen video and keyboard and mouse actions.  The launch of ‌Anthropic's Claude Cowork, which ⁠can perform a range of computer-based tasks autonomously, has already spooked software investors who fear that agentic AI ⁠can disrupt established business models.

raw.headline: Musk unveils joint Tesla-xAI project 'Macrohard', eyes software disruption
raw.summary: Elon Musk on Wednesday unveiled a joint project between Tesla and his artificial intelligence startup xAI, which ‌he called "Macrohard" or "Digital Optimus" and said that it was a ‌system capable of emulating the functions of software companies.  In a post on his social ​media platform X, Musk said the project pairs xAI's Grok large language model, acting as a high-level "navigator," with a Tesla-developed AI agent that processes real-time computer screen video and keyboard and mouse actions.  The launch of ‌Anthropic's Claude Cowork, which ⁠can perform a range of computer-based tasks autonomously, has already spooked software investors who fear that agentic AI ⁠can disrupt established business models.

headline: Oracle Gets Customers To Start Chipping In—Heard on the Street
summary: Worries about Oracle’s ability to pay for all the AI deals it has struck cost the company more than half its market value over the past six months.  Getting customers to shoulder some of the load is repairing some of that damage.  Revenue and earnings for the February-ending period beat Wall Street’s estimates, and the company even managed to grow its substantial book of expected future revenue: Remaining performance obligations rose by $30 billion to hit $553 billion.

raw.headline: Oracle Gets Customers To Start Chipping In—Heard on the Street
raw.summary: Worries about Oracle’s ability to pay for all the AI deals it has struck cost the company more than half its market value over the past six months.  Getting customers to shoulder some of the load is repairing some of that damage.  Revenue and earnings for the February-ending period beat Wall Street’s estimates, and the company even managed to grow its substantial book of expected future revenue: Remaining performance obligations rose by $30 billion to hit $553 billion.

headline: Anthropic Tells Judge Billions at Stake If US Shuns AI Tool
summary: The startup made a case for urgency to US District Judge Rita F. Lin at a hearing in San Francisco a day after Anthropic sued the Defense Department over the supply-risk designation.  Microsoft Corp., which owns a stake in OpenAI and Anthropic, meanwhile filed its own brief urging a judge to temporarily block the government’s moves because they have the potential to delay all ongoing Defense Department “contracting for IT products and services.”  Michael Mongan, an attorney for Anthropic, argued Tuesday that the federal government’s actions have led to more than 100 enterprise customers contacting the company to express doubt about continuing their work with Anthropic.

raw.headline: Anthropic Tells Judge Billions at Stake If US Shuns AI Tool
raw.summary: The startup made a case for urgency to US District Judge Rita F. Lin at a hearing in San Francisco a day after Anthropic sued the Defense Department over the supply-risk designation.  Microsoft Corp., which owns a stake in OpenAI and Anthropic, meanwhile filed its own brief urging a judge to temporarily block the government’s moves because they have the potential to delay all ongoing Defense Department “contracting for IT products and services.”  Michael Mongan, an attorney for Anthropic, argued Tuesday that the federal government’s actions have led to more than 100 enterprise customers contacting the company to express doubt about continuing their work with Anthropic.

headline: Oracle sees AI boom through at least 2027, sending shares up 8%
summary: STORY: :: OracleOracle on Tuesday predicted that the AI data center boom will power its revenue above Wall Street estimates well into 2027.That sent shares in the firm up over 8% in extended trading.Oracle reported third quarter revenue of just over $17 billion, beating estimates.It expects fourth-quarter revenue growth of up to 21%.Tuesday's results helped allay investor concerns over Oracle's costly multi-billion dollar push into AI computing.:: Oracle:: FileThe company has made a dramatic turn toward building data centers for partners such as OpenAI and Meta.At the same time, it enacted layoffs as it uses smaller engineering teams and AI coding tools to roll out new software.:: Oracle:: FileOracle has also been aggressively spending to expand its cloud infrastructure to support generative AI, competing against hyperscalers such as Amazon's AWS and Microsoft's Azure.On a conference call with investors, CEO Clay Magouyrk said that the company's margins on its cloud business should improve over time.

raw.headline: Oracle sees AI boom through at least 2027, sending shares up 8%

headline: Microsoft Warns Of Costly Disruptions As It Backs Anthropic Bid To Block Department Of War Supply-Chain Risk Designation
summary: Microsoft backed Anthropic in court with an amicus brief seeking to block the U.S. Department of Defense from labeling the AI startup a supply-chain risk, warning the move could disrupt defense contractors relying on its technology.
